core_intuitions: Attention cost is quadratic in T (score matrix and FLOPs), and activation/KV memory grows linearly with T per layer — so long context is fundamentally a memory-and-arithmetic problem, not just a config change.

Standard RoPE extrapolates poorly because when position m exceeds T_train, the high-frequency (fast-rotating, short-range) dimensions enter phase angles the model never saw during training, producing pathological attention scores.

The PI / NTK-aware / YaRN family are three different ways to remap RoPE frequencies into the trained angular range: PI compresses ALL frequencies uniformly (hurting local resolution), NTK raises the base to stretch the rope, and YaRN blends per-frequency — leaving high-freq local dims untouched and scaling only low-freq global dims.

Naive document packing lets tokens attend across unrelated document boundaries; a block-diagonal causal mask (realised cheaply via FlashAttention varlen / cu_seqlens) confines attention to within each document.

Ring attention shards the sequence across GPUs in a ring and rotates KV chunks hop by hop; because FlashAttention's online-softmax accumulation is associative, this reconstructs the EXACT full-attention output with no approximation, overlapping the KV transfer with compute.
Causal masking in a ring creates load imbalance: fully-future KV blocks are skipped, so rank 0 processes 1 block while rank N-1 processes N.

new_figures.name: longctx-rope-extension-spectrum
new_figures.concept: How PI, NTK-aware, and YaRN each remap RoPE frequencies into the trained angular range, and why YaRN protects local resolution while extending global range.
new_figures.rationale: This is the mathematical heart of the chapter and the single hardest idea to grasp from prose: that RoPE dimensions form a frequency spectrum, that the failure is specifically at the high-frequency end going out-of-distribution, and that the three fix methods differ precisely in HOW they treat each frequency. A reader who sees the spectrum-and-treatment picture understands PI vs NTK vs YaRN in one glance; the equations alone leave it abstract.
new_figures.placement: After the '!!! example "Worked Example: Comparing PI vs YaRN angle ranges at 8x extension"' block at the end of the YaRN section (before the '---' that precedes 'Continued Pretraining on Long Documents').
new_figures.brief:
A two-panel static infographic on a theme-neutral background (use CSS variables / currentColor, never hardcoded primaries; convey categories with fill patterns, stroke weight, and neutral tints plus one accent).

TOP PANEL — 'Why standard RoPE breaks at long positions'. Draw a horizontal frequency spectrum bar spanning the panel, labelled on the left 'high-frequency dims (i=0): short wavelength, fast rotation, LOCAL / short-range' and on the right 'low-frequency dims (large i): long wavelength, slow rotation, GLOBAL / long-range'. Above the bar place 3 small 'clock' dials sampled at low, mid, high i. Draw a shaded 'trained angular range' band (positions 0..T_train). For each clock show two hands: a solid hand for the max angle seen in training and a dashed/red-accent hand for the angle at the extended target position. The high-frequency dial's extended hand has wound far past the trained band into a hatched 'unseen phase' zone (label 'out of distribution -> pathological scores'); the low-frequency dial has barely moved even at the target position (label 'barely rotates — under-uses its range'). Use small illustrative integers only if needed (e.g. angle ~4096 rad vs ~32768 rad as text callouts, matching the chapter's worked example) — invent no benchmark numbers.

BOTTOM PANEL — 'Three ways to fit the target window into the trained range'. Reuse the SAME horizontal high-freq -> low-freq spectrum axis, and stack three labelled rows sharing that axis. Each row shows the per-dimension effective scaling applied (a curve or shaded profile over the spectrum), plus a one-line caption:
  Row 'Position Interpolation (PI)': a FLAT profile at full 1/s scaling across the whole spectrum. Caption: 'compresses ALL frequencies equally -> high-freq dims now too slow, adjacent tokens (dist 1) collapse to ~1/8 apart'.
  Row 'NTK-aware (base rescaling)': a gently RISING profile — little effective scaling at high-freq, growing toward low-freq (because raising base b compounds with i). Caption: 'raise the base b -> stretch the rope; keeps local resolution, often works zero-shot'.
  Row 'YaRN (per-frequency ramp)': an explicit RAMP — a protected flat 'no scaling' plateau on the high-freq (local) side up to a beta_fast boundary, then a smooth ramp up to full PI scaling on the low-freq (global) side beyond beta_slow. Shade/annotate the two regions: 'PROTECTED: local dims untouched' and 'SCALED: global dims stretched to reach far tokens'. Caption: 'best of both — plus a small softmax temperature correction'.

Keep a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order; align all three bottom rows to the same x-axis so the reader can compare the profiles vertically. Everything must fit inside the viewBox with no overlapping labels. Legible in both light and dark themes.

new_figures.name: longctx-ring-attention-rotation
new_figures.concept: Sequence sharded across N GPUs in a ring; KV chunks rotate hop-by-hop while each GPU accumulates its Q chunk's exact attention output via associative online softmax.
new_figures.rationale: Ring attention is the systems core of the chapter and is genuinely a multi-step process: the intuition that a fixed Q chunk sees every KV chunk over N-1 rotations, that communication overlaps compute, and that the online-softmax merge yields the EXACT result, cannot be captured in a single static frame the way motion captures it. Seeing the KV blocks circulate and the accumulators grow is what makes 'no approximation' click. It also naturally shows the causal load imbalance.
new_figures.placement: In the 'Ring Attention' subsection, immediately after the fenced 'Ring of 4 GPUs' text-diagram block (before the 'The key algorithmic insight...' paragraph).
new_figures.brief:
A long-animation of ring/context parallel attention over N=4 GPUs, fully understandable as a single static frame (the animation only advances the rotation counter). Theme-safe colors only (currentColor + neutral tints + one accent for the moving KV; no hardcoded primaries).

LAYOUT: Four GPU nodes arranged around a ring (corners or circle). Each node card shows, fixed in place, its own Query chunk Q_i (label 'Q0'..'Q3', these NEVER move) and two running accumulators: an Output tile O_i and a log-sum-exp scalar l_i / running-max m_i (label 'online-softmax state'). A directional ring arrow connects node i -> i+1 (mod N) showing the KV travel direction. The full sequence bar [chunk0 | chunk1 | chunk2 | chunk3] appears once at the top as a legend so the reader sees the sequence was split into contiguous chunks, one per GPU.

MOVING ELEMENT: a KV block token (label 'KV_j') sits at each node and rotates one hop per step. Color/outline it as the accent 'in-flight' object.

STEPS (animation frames, with a visible 'round k / N' counter and a static caption strip explaining the current round):
  Step 0: each GPU computes attention of Q_i against its OWN KV_i (diagonal block). Show a small 'compute' pulse on each node and the accumulator initialising.
  Step 1..N-1: KV chunks slide one hop along the ring arrows ('KV in flight — overlaps with compute'); each node then computes Q_i against the newly arrived KV_j and MERGES into its accumulator. Depict the merge as: new partial (o_new, l_new) combined with running (O_i, l_i) via 'rescale by exp(m_old - m_new)' — a tiny inline formula or two-bar 'rescale + add' glyph — with the caption 'associative: exact same result as full attention, no approximation'.
  Final frame: every Q_i has now seen all 4 KV chunks; label each node 'complete: full softmax output for Q_i'.

CAUSAL SKIP / IMBALANCE annotation: shade KV blocks that are entirely in the future of a node's queries as 'skipped (fully-future, causal)'. Make visible that rank 0 ends up doing 1 block of real work while rank N-1 does N — a small side bar-chart or per-node work tally captioned 'causal load imbalance (zigzag/striped ring rebalances this)'.

Overlay one persistent caption: 'communication (KV rotation) overlaps computation'. Use only small illustrative integers (N=4, T/N chunks). The static first frame alone must convey: fixed Q per GPU, KV rotates around the ring, each GPU accumulates online-softmax state. No overlapping labels; everything inside the viewBox; legible in light and dark.

new_figures.name: longctx-doc-packing-mask
new_figures.concept: Block-diagonal causal masking confines attention to within each packed document, preventing cross-document contamination, and is realised at zero storage cost via FlashAttention varlen / cu_seqlens.
new_figures.rationale: Document packing is easy to state but its failure mode (cross-document attention garbage, which dominates the window at 128K) and its fix (block-diagonal vs full causal mask) are inherently spatial — a T x T mask picture makes both the problem and the solution instantly legible in a way the code listing does not. It also ties the cu_seqlens API and loss-masking to a single visual, anchoring an otherwise abstract 'varlen' concept.
new_figures.placement: In the 'Document Masking in Attention' subsection, right after the paragraph ending 'This is equivalent to block-diagonal causal masking over the packed sequence.' (before the build_packed_attention_mask code block).
new_figures.brief:
A static three-part infographic showing packed-document attention masking. Theme-safe (currentColor, neutral tints, one accent for the 'contamination' highlight; distinguish allowed vs blocked cells by fill pattern AND tint so it survives grayscale). Use a small illustrative packed sequence of 3 documents with tiny lengths, e.g. Doc A = 4 tokens, Doc B = 3, Doc C = 3 (total 10) — small integers only.

LEFT — the packed sequence: a horizontal strip of 10 token cells, grouped and tinted into three labelled segments [Doc A | Doc B | Doc C], with the cumulative-length markers annotated as 'cu_seqlens = [0, 4, 7, 10]' to concretely link the picture to the varlen API.

MIDDLE — 'Naive causal mask (WRONG)': a 10x10 grid, rows = query token, cols = key token, with the full lower-triangular region marked 'attend'. Highlight in the accent/warning color the off-diagonal below-diagonal blocks where Doc B and Doc C queries attend back into Doc A / Doc B keys, labelled 'cross-document attention -> unrelated tokens, contamination (dominates the window at long context)'. Add a small arrow/callout: 'first token of Doc B attends to end of Doc A'.

RIGHT — 'Block-diagonal causal mask (RIGHT)': the same 10x10 grid but only three lower-triangular blocks ON the diagonal (4x4, 3x3, 3x3) are 'attend'; all off-diagonal blocks are cleared/blocked. Label 'each token attends only within its own document, still causal'. Add a caption strip beneath both grids: 'FlashAttention varlen (cu_seqlens) computes exactly the RIGHT mask without ever materialising a T x T tensor — O(1) mask storage'.

BELOW everything, a thin 'loss mask' row aligned under the LEFT sequence strip: 10 cells of 0/1 where the FIRST token of each document is 0 (no loss) and the rest are 1, captioned 'loss mask: do not predict a document''s first token from the previous document'.

Clear left-to-right reading order; grid cells large enough to read the diagonal structure; no overlapping labels; fits the viewBox; legible in both themes.

summary: This concept-rich chapter had zero figures. I propose three foundational visuals, no audits needed. (1) longctx-rope-extension-spectrum [static, P1] — the mathematical heart: a frequency-spectrum view of why standard RoPE breaks at the high-frequency end when m>T_train, and how PI (uniform compression), NTK (base rescaling), and YaRN (per-frequency ramp) each remap frequencies differently; this single figure makes the whole PI/NTK/YaRN arc click. (2) longctx-ring-attention-rotation [long-animation, P1] — the systems heart: KV chunks rotating around a ring of GPUs while fixed Q chunks accumulate exact attention via associative online softmax, with comm/compute overlap and the causal load-imbalance made visible; motion is genuinely essential to convey the N-1 rotations reconstructing full attention with no approximation. (3) longctx-doc-packing-mask [static, P2] — a T x T mask picture contrasting naive cross-document contamination against block-diagonal causal masking (tied to cu_seqlens/varlen and the loss mask). I deliberately omitted figures for the quadratic-cost table (already well conveyed in prose/table) and the NIAH heatmap (the chapter's plotting code and description suffice, and it is an eval artifact rather than a core mechanism).
